sourceQuestion:
A grammar G is said to be in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) if all its productions are of the form A -> BC or A -> a, where A, B and C are non-terminals and a is a terminal. Suppose G is a CFG in CNF and w is a string in L(G) of length m, then how long is a derivation of w in G?

A. 2m - 1
B. 2m
C. 2m + 1
D. m

parsed.stem: A grammar G is said to be in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) if all its productions are of the form A -> BC or A -> a, where A, B and C are non-terminals and a is a terminal. Suppose G is a CFG in CNF and w is a string in L(G) of length m, then how long is a derivation of w in G?

sourceQuestion:
Match the following items:

(i)   Backus-Naur form          (a) Regular expressions
(ii)  Lexical analysis           (b) LALR(1) grammars
(iii) YACC                       (c) CNF grammars
(iv)  Recursive descent parsing  (d) General context-free grammars

A. i-d, ii-a, iii-b, iv-c
B. i-d, ii-a, iii-c, iv-b
C. i-a, ii-d, iii-b, iv-c
D. i-d, ii-c, iii-a, iv-b

parsed.stem:
Match the following items:

(i)   Backus-Naur form          (a) Regular expressions
(ii)  Lexical analysis           (b) LALR(1) grammars
(iii) YACC                       (c) CNF grammars
(iv)  Recursive descent parsing  (d) General context-free grammars

sourceQuestion:
Consider a grammar with the following productions:
S -> aab | loc B
A -> aA | b
B -> bB | epsilon

Based on the image, the GATE 1995 grammar question (3.8.11) shows productions:
S -> aab | loc B
with various options. From GATE 1995 official question bank, the question is:

Consider a grammar with the following productions:
- S -> aab | b
- A -> aA | b

Which of the following grammars generates the same language?

A. S -> aS | b
B. S -> bS | a
C. S -> aS | ab
D. None of the above

- A -> aA | b

Which of the following grammars generates the same language?

sourceQuestion:
The grammar whose productions are

<stmt> -> if <expr> then <stmt> | if <expr> then <stmt> else <stmt> | <id> := <id>

is ambiguous because

A. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' has more than two parse trees
B. the left most and right most derivations of the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' give rise to different parse trees
C. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has more than one parse tree
D. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees
sourceAnswer: D. the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees

parsed.stem:
The grammar whose productions are

<stmt> -> if <expr> then <stmt> | if <expr> then <stmt> else <stmt> | <id> := <id>

is ambiguous because

options.text: the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' has more than two parse trees
the left most and right most derivations of the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d' give rise to different parse trees
the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has more than one parse tree
the sentence 'if a then if b then c := d else c := d' has two parse trees

sourceQuestion:
In the following grammar:
  X ::= X @ Y | Y
  Y ::= Z * Y | Z
  Z ::= id

Which of the following is true?
A. @ is left associative while * is right associative
B. Both @ and * are left associative
C. @ is right associative while * is left associative
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: A. @ is left associative while * is right associative

parsed.stem:
In the following grammar:
  X ::= X @ Y | Y
  Y ::= Z * Y | Z
  Z ::= id

Which of the following is true?

options.text: @ is left associative while * is right associative
@ is right associative while * is left associative

sourceQuestion:
A grammar that is both left and right recursive for a non-terminal, is
A. Ambiguous
B. Unambiguous
C. Information is not sufficient to decide whether it is ambiguous or unambiguous
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. Information is not sufficient to decide whether it is ambiguous or unambiguous

parsed.stem: A grammar that is both left and right recursive for a non-terminal, is

options.text: Information is not sufficient to decide whether it is ambiguous or unambiguous

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. An unambiguous grammar has same leftmost and rightmost derivation
B. An LL(1) parser is a top-down parser
C. LALR is more powerful than SLR
D. An ambiguous grammar can never be LR(k) for any k
sourceAnswer: A. An unambiguous grammar has same leftmost and rightmost derivation

LALR is more powerful than SLR
An ambiguous grammar can never be LR(k) for any k

sourceQuestion:
Consider the translation scheme shown below.

S  -> TR
R  -> +T { print('+'); } R | epsilon
T  -> num { print(num.val); }

Here num is a token that represents an integer and num.val represents the corresponding integer value. For an input string '9+5+2', this translation scheme will print

A. 9+5+2
B. 9 5 + 2 +
C. 9 5 2 + +
D. + + 9 5 2

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following grammar rules violate the requirements of an operator grammar? P, Q, R, S are non-terminals, and a, b are terminals.

I.   P -> QR
II.  P -> aQbR
III. P -> epsilon
IV.  P -> a Q b R a

A. (I) only
B. (I) and (III) only
C. (II) and (III) only
D. (III) and (IV) only

parsed.stem:

II.  P -> aQbR
III. P -> epsilon
IV.  P -> a Q b R a

Consider the following grammar G:
S -> bA | aB
A -> bAA | aS | a
B -> aBB | bS | b
Let N_a(w) and N_b(w) denote the number of a's and b's in a string w respectively. The language L(G) over {a,b}* generated by G is:

sourceQuestion:
The grammar A -> AA | (A) | epsilon is not suitable for predictive-parsing because the grammar is:
A. ambiguous
B. left-recursive
C. right-recursive
D. an operator-grammar

parsed.stem: The grammar A -> AA |

options.text: | epsilon is not suitable for predictive-parsing because the grammar is: A. ambiguous

sourceQuestion:
Consider the grammar:
E -> E + n | E x n | n
For a sentence n + n x n, the handles in the right-sentential form of the reduction are:
A. n + n and n x n
B. n + n and x n
C. n + n x n and + n
D. n and + n

parsed.stem:
Consider the grammar:
E -> E + n | E x n | n
For a sentence n + n x n, the handles in the right-sentential form of the reduction are:

options.text: n + n and n x n
n + n x n and + n

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following statements about the context free grammar G = {S -> SS, S -> aS, S -> Sa, S -> epsilon}:
I. G is ambiguous
II. G produces all strings with equal number of a's and b's
Which combination below expresses all the true statements about G?
A. I only
B. II only
C. I and III only
D. I, II and III

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following translation scheme:
  S -> ER
  R -> E { print('+', n) } R | epsilon
  E -> id { print(id.val) }
Here id is a token that represents an integer and id.val represents the corresponding integer value. For an input '2+3+4', this translation scheme prints:
A. 2 3 + 4 +
B. 2 + 3 4 +
C. 2 3 4 + +
D. 2 3 4 + +

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following grammars generates the language L = {a^i b^j | i != j}?
A. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> bB | b
B. S -> aS | Sb | a | b
C. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | epsilon
   B -> Bb | epsilon
D. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> Bb | b
sourceAnswer:
D. S -> AC | CB
   C -> aCb | epsilon
   A -> aA | a
   B -> Bb | b

parsed.stem: Which one of the following grammars generates the language L = {a^i b^j | i != j}?

options.text: S -> AC | CB C -> aCb | epsilon A -> aA | a B -> bB | b
S -> AC | CB C -> aCb | epsilon A -> aA | epsilon B -> Bb | epsilon
S -> AC | CB C -> aCb | epsilon A -> aA | a B -> Bb | b

sourceQuestion:
The grammar
  S -> AC | CB
  C -> aCb | epsilon
  A -> aA | a
  B -> Bb | b
generates the language L = {a^i b^j | i != j}. In this grammar, what is the length of the derivation (number of steps starting from S) to generate the string a^m b^n with m > n?
A. max(m, n) + 2
B. m + n + 2
C. l = m + 3
D. max(l, m) + 3

The grammar is NOT LL(1) because:
A. it is left recursive
B. it is right recursive
C. it is ambiguous
D. it is not context-free

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two statements:
P: Every regular grammar is LL(1)
Q: Every regular set has a LR(1) grammar

Which of the following is TRUE?
A. Both P and Q are true
B. P is true and Q is false
C. P is false and Q is true
D. Both P and Q are false
sourceAnswer: C. P is false and Q is true

parsed.stem:

Q: Every regular set has a LR(1) grammar

Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Both P and Q are true
P is true and Q is false
P is false and Q is true
Both P and Q are false

A -> aS

B -> b

Which of the following strings is generated by the grammar?
A. aaabb
B. aabbb
C. aabbb
D. abbb

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements are true?
I. Every left-recursive grammar can be converted to a right-recursive grammar and vice-versa
II. All epsilon-productions can be removed from any context-free grammar by suitable transformations
III. The language generated by a context-free grammar all of whose productions are of the form X -> w or X -> wY (where w is a string of terminals and Y is a non-terminal), is always regular
IV. The derivation trees of strings generated by a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form are always binary trees

A. I, II, III and IV
B. II, III and IV only
C. I, II and IV only
D. I, II and IV only
sourceAnswer: A. I, II, III and IV

parsed.stem:
Which of the following statements are true?
I. Every left-recursive grammar can be converted to a right-recursive grammar and vice-versa
II. All epsilon-productions can be removed from any context-free grammar by suitable transformations
III. The language generated by a context-free grammar all of whose productions are of the form X -> w or X -> wY (where w is a string of terminals and Y is a non-terminal), is always regular
IV. The derivation trees of strings generated by a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form are always binary trees

sourceQuestion:
The language generated by a context-free grammar all of whose productions are of the form X -> w or X -> wY (where w is a string of terminals and Y is a non-terminal) is always regular. The derivation trees of strings generated by a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form are always binary trees.

The grammar S -> eSa | AS | c a

A. I, II and IV
B. II, III and IV only
C. I, II and IV only
D. I, II and IV only

Note: The exact question concerns which of statements I–IV are correct. Based on GATE 2010 (q about grammar properties), the answer is option D. I, II and IV only.
sourceAnswer: D. I, II and IV only

parsed.stem:
The language generated by a context-free grammar all of whose productions are of the form X -> w or X -> wY (where w is a string of terminals and Y is a non-terminal) is always regular. The derivation trees of strings generated by a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form are always binary trees.

The grammar S -> eSa | AS | c a

options.text: I, II and IV only Note: The exact question concerns which of statements I–IV are correct. Based on GATE 2010 (q about grammar properties), the answer is option D. I, II and IV only.

sourceQuestion:
Consider the grammar defined by the following production rules, with two operators * and +:

S -> T * P
T -> U | T + U
Q -> P + Q | P
P -> Q * P | Q
Q -> id
U -> id

Which one of the following is TRUE?

A. + is left associative, while * is right associative
B. + is right associative, while * is left associative
C. Both + and * are right associative
D. Both + and * are left associative
sourceAnswer: A. + is left associative, while * is right associative

parsed.stem:
Consider the grammar defined by the following production rules, with two operators * and +:

S -> T * P
T -> U | T + U
Q -> P + Q | P
P -> Q * P | Q
Q -> id
U -> id

Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: + is left associative, while * is right associative
+ is right associative, while * is left associative

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following grammars is free from left recursion?

A. S -> AB | b
   A -> Bd | b
   B -> (no production given in options)

B. S -> TE'
   E' -> +TE' | epsilon
   T -> FT'
   T' -> *FT' | epsilon
   F -> (E) | id

C. S -> Aa | b
   A -> Ac | Sd | epsilon

D. S -> Aa | b
   A -> b | epsilon
sourceAnswer: B. S -> TE'; E' -> +TE' | epsilon; T -> FT'; T' -> *FT' | epsilon; F -> (E) | id

parsed.stem: Which one of the following grammars is free from left recursion?

options.text: S -> AB | b A -> Bd | b B -> (no production given in options)
S -> TE' E' -> +TE' | epsilon T -> FT' T' -> *FT' | epsilon F -> (E) | id
S -> Aa | b A -> Ac | Sd | epsilon
S -> Aa | b A -> b | epsilon

sourceQuestion:
A student wrote two context-free grammars G1 and G2 for generating a single C-like array declaration. The dimension of the array is at least one. For example, int a[10][3];

The grammars use D as the start symbol, and use six terminal symbols: int ; id [ ] num.

Grammar G1:
D -> int L;
L -> L id E
E -> [num] E | [num]

Grammar G2:
D -> int L;
L -> id E
E -> [num] E | epsilon

Which of the grammars correctly generates the declaration mentioned above?

A. Both G1 and G2
B. Only G1
C. Only G2
D. Neither G1 nor G2

parsed.stem:
A student wrote two context-free grammars G1 and G2 for generating a single C-like array declaration. The dimension of the array is at least one. For example, int a[10][3];

The grammars use D as the start symbol, and use six terminal symbols: int ; id [ ] num.

Grammar G1:
D -> int L;
L -> L id E
E -> [num] E | [num]

Grammar G2:
D -> int L;
L -> id E
E -> [num] E | epsilon

Which of the grammars correctly generates the declaration mentioned above?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following expression grammar G:
E -> E - T | T
T -> T * F | F
F -> (E) | id

Which of the following grammars is not left recursive, but is equivalent to G?

A. E -> E - T | T
   T -> T * F' | F'
   F' -> E | id
   F -> (E') | id

B. E -> T E'
   E' -> -T E' | epsilon
   T -> F T'
   T' -> *F T' | epsilon
   F -> (E) | id

C. E -> TX
   X -> -TX | epsilon
   T -> FY
   Y -> *FY | epsilon
   F -> (E) | id

D. E -> TX | (TX)
   X -> -TX | epsilon
   T -> FY | id
   Y -> *FY | epsilon
   F -> (E) | id
sourceAnswer: C. E -> TX, X -> -TX | epsilon, T -> FY, Y -> *FY | epsilon, F -> (E) | id

parsed.stem:
Consider the following expression grammar G:
E -> E - T | T
T -> T * F | F
F -> (E) | id

Which of the following grammars is not left recursive, but is equivalent to G?

options.text: E -> E - T | T T -> T * F' | F' F' -> E | id F -> (E') | id
E -> T E' E' -> -T E' | epsilon T -> F T' T' -> *F T' | epsilon F -> (E) | id
E -> TX X -> -TX | epsilon T -> FY Y -> *FY | epsilon F -> (E) | id
E -> TX | (TX) X -> -TX | epsilon T -> FY | id Y -> *FY | epsilon F ->

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following options gives the correct matches of items in Column 1 with their items in Column 2?

The following parsing table is partially filled ("blank" denotes that the corresponding cell is empty):

        a       b       c       d       f       $
S       1       S->Rf   3       T->bT   2       
T       T->aS   T->bT           3       blank   
R               2               R->aTR  R->e    

Which one of the following options correctly matches the blank cells?
A. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] T->e, [4] blank
B. [2] S->Rf, [3] blank, [1] T->e
C. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] blank, [4] T->e
D. [1] S->Rf, [2] blank, [3] T->e
